layout_dets.text: Abstract

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Information technology (IT) acceptance research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: has yielded many competing models, each with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: different sets of acceptance determinants. In this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: paper, we (1) review user acceptance literature

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and discuss eight prominent models, (2) empiri-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cally compare the eight models and their exten-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sions, (3) formulate a unified model that integrates

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: elements across the eight models, and (4) empiri-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cally validate the unified model. The eight models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reviewed are the theory of reasoned action, the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: technology acceptance model, the motivational

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: model, the theory of planned behavior, a model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: combining the technology acceptance model and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the theory of planned behavior, the model of PC

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: utilization, the innovation diffusion theory, and the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: social cognitive theory. Using data from four

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: organizations over a six-month period with three

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: points of measurement, the eight models ex-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plained between 17 percent and 53 percent of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: variance in user intentions to use information

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: technology. Next, a unified model, called the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Tech-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nology (UTAUT), was formulated, with four core

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: determinants of intention and usage, and up to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: four moderators of key relationships. UTAUT was

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: then tested using the original data and found to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: outperform the eight individual models (adjusted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: R² of 69 percent). UTAUT was then confirmed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with data from two new organizations with similar

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: results (adjusted R² of 70 percent). UTAUT thus

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: provides a useful tool for managers needing to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Cynthia Beath was the accepting senior editor for this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: paper.

layout_dets.text: Introduction

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The presence of computer and information tech-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nologies in today's organizations has expanded

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dramatically. Some estimates indicate that, since

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the 1980s, about 50 percent of all new capital

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: investment in organizations has been in informa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion technology (Westland and Clark 2000). Yet,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for technologies to improve productivity, they must

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be accepted and used by employees in organi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: zations. Explaining user acceptance of new tech-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nology is often described as one of the most

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mature research areas in the contemporary infor-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mation systems (IS) literature (e.g., Hu et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1999). Research in this area has resulted in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: several theoretical models, with roots in informa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion systems, psychology, and sociology, that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: routinely explain over 40 percent of the variance in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: individual intention to use technology (e.g., Davis

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: et al. 1989; Taylor and Todd 1995b; Venkatesh

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Davis 2000). Researchers are confronted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with a choice among a multitude of models and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: find that they must "pick and choose" constructs

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: across the models, or choose a "favored model"

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and largely ignore the contributions from

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: alternative models. Thus, there is a need for a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: review and synthesis in order to progress toward

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a unified view of user acceptance.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The current work has the following objectives:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1) To review the extant user acceptance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models: The primary purpose of this review

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is to assess the current state of knowledge

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with respect to understanding individual

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: acceptance of new information technologies.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: This review identifies eight prominent models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and discusses their similarities and dif-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ferences. Some authors have previously ob-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: served some of the similarities across

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models.² However, our review is the first to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: assess similarities and differences across all

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: eight models, a necessary first step toward

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the ultimate goal of the paper: the develop-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ment of a unified theory of individual accep-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tance of technology. The review is presented

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: in the following section.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (2) To empirically compare the eight models:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: We conduct a within-subjects, longitudinal

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: validation and comparison of the eight

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models using data from four organizations.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: This provides a baseline assessment of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: relative explanatory power of the individual

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models against which the unified model can

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be compared. The empirical model compari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: son is presented in the third section.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ()To formulate the Unified Theory of Accep-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tance and Use of Technology (UTAUT):

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Based upon conceptual and empirical simi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: larities across models, we formulate a unified

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: model. The formulation of UTAUT is pre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sented in the fourth section.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: To empirically validate UTAUT: An empirical

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: test of UTAUT on the original data provides

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: preliminary support for our contention that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: UTAUT outperforms each of the eight original

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models. UTAUT is then cross-validated using

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: data from two new organizations. The empiri-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cal validation of UTAUT is presented in the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fifth section.

layout_dets.text: Acceptance Models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: There have been many tests of the eight models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: but there have only been four studies reporting

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: empirically-based comparisons of two or more of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the eight models published in the major informa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion systems journals. Table 3 provides a brief

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: overview of each of the model comparison

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: studies. Despite the apparent maturity of the re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: search stream, a comprehensive comparison of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the key competing models has not been con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ducted in a single study. Below, we identify five

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: limitations of these prior model tests and compari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sons, and how we address these limitations in our

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: work.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models and defines their theorized determinants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of intention and/or usage. The models hypo-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: thesize between two and seven determinants of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: acceptance, for a total of 32 constructs across

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the eight models. Table 2 identifies four key

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: moderating variables (experience, voluntariness,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gender, and age) that have been found to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant in conjunction with these models.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Technology studied: The technologies that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: have been studied in many of the model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: development and comparison studies have

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: been relatively simple, individual-oriented

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: information technologies as opposed to more

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: complex and sophisticated organizational

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: technologies that are the focus of managerial

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: concern and of this study.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Constructs

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Prior Model Tests and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Model Comparisons

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IS research has long studied how and why indivi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: duals adopt new information technologies. Within

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: this broad area of inquiry, there have been several

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: streams of research. One stream of research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: focuses on individual acceptance of technology by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: using intention or usage as a dependent variable

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (e.g., Compeau and Higgins 1995b; Davis et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Other streams have focused on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: implementation success at the organizational level

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Leonard-Barton and Deschamps 1988) and task-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: technology fit (Goodhue 1995; Goodhue and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thompson 1995), among others. While each of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: these streams makes important and unique

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: contributions to the literature on user acceptance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of information technology, the theoretical models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to be included in the present review, comparison,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and synthesis employ intention and/or usage as

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the key dependent variable. The goal here is to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: understand usage as the dependent variable. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: role of intention as a predictor of behavior (e.g.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: usage) is critical and has been well-established in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IS and the reference disciplines (see Ajzen 1991;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Sheppard et al. 1988; Taylor and Todd 1995b).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Figure 1 presents the basic conceptual framework

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: underlying the class of models explaining indivi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dual acceptance of information technology that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: forms the basis of this research. Our review re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sulted in the identification of eight key competing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: theoretical models. Table 1 describes the eight

Table 1. Models and Theories of Individual AcceptanceTheory of Reasoned Action (TRA) Core Constructs Definitions
Theory of Reasoned Action (TRA)Core Constructs
Drawn from social psychology, TRA is one of the mostfundamental and influential theories of human behavior.It has been used to predict a wide range of behaviors(see Sheppard et al. 1988 for a review). Davis et al.(1989) applied TRA to individual acceptance of techno-logy and found that the variance explained was largelyconsistent with studies that had employed TRA in thecontext of other behaviors.Attitude TowardBehavior"an individual's positive or negative feelings (evaluativeaffect) about performing the target behavior" (Fishbein andAjzen 1975, p. 216).
Subjective Norm"the person's perception that most people who areimportant to him think he should or should not perform thebehavior in question" (Fishbein and Ajzen 1975, p. 302).
Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
TAM is tailored to IS contexts, and was designed to pre-dict information technology acceptance and usage onthe job. Unlike TRA, the final conceptualization of TAMexcludes the attitude construct in order to better explainintention parsimoniously. TAM2 extended TAM by in-cluding subjective norm as an additional predictor ofintention in the case of mandatory settings (Venkateshand Davis 2000). TAM has been widely applied to adiverse set of technologies and users.Perceived Usefulness"the degree to which a person believes that using aparticular system would enhance his or her jobperformance" (Davis 1989, p. 320).
Perceived Ease ofUse"the degree to which a person believes that using aparticular system would be free of effort" (Davis 1989, p.320).
Subjective NormAdapted from TRA/TPB. Included in TAM2 only.
Motivational Model (MM)
A significant body of research in psychology has sup-ported general motivation theory as an explanation forbehavior. Several studies have examined motivationaltheory and adapted it for specific contexts. Vallerand(1997) presents an excellent review of the fundamentalExtrinsic MotivationThe perception that users will want to perform an activity"because it is perceived to be instrumental in achievingvalued outcomes that are distinct from the activity itself,such as improved job performance, pay, or promotions"(Davis et al. 1992, p. 1112).
tenets of this theoretical base. Within the informationsystems domain, Davis et al. (1992) applied motiva-tional theory to understand new technology adoptionand use (see also Venkatesh and Speier 1999).Intrinsic MotivationThe perception that users will want to perform an activity"for no apparent reinforcement other than the process ofperforming the activity per se" (Davis et al. 1992, p. 1112).

Table 1. Models and Theories of Individual Acceptance (Continued)Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) Core Constructs Definitions
Core Constructs
TPB extended TRA by adding the construct of perceivedbehavioral control. In TPB, perceived behavioral controlis theorized to be an additional determinant of intentionand behavior. Ajzen (1991) presented a review ofseveral studies that successfully used TPB to predictintention and behavior in a wide variety of settings. TPBhas been successfully applied to the understanding ofindividual acceptance and usage of many different tech-nologies (Harrison et al. 1997; Mathieson 1991; Taylorand Todd 1995b). A related model is the DecomposedTheory of Planned Behavior (DTPB). In terms of pre-dicting intention, DTPB is identical to TPB. In contrastto TPB but similar to TAM, DTPB "decomposes" atti-tude, subjective norm, and perceived behavioral controlinto its the underlying belief structure within technologyadoption contexts.Attitude TowardBehaviorAdapted from TRA.
Subjective NormAdapted from TRA.
Perceived BehavioralControlthe perceived ease or difficulty of performing thebehavior" (Ajzen 1991, p. 188). In the context of ISresearch, "perceptions of internal and external constraintson behavior" (Taylor and Todd 1995b, p. 149).
Combined TAM and TPB (C-TAM-TPB)
This model combines the predictors of TPB withperceived usefulness from TAM to provide a hybridmodel (Taylor and Todd 1995a).Attitude TowardBehaviorAdapted from TRA/TPB.
Subjective NormAdapted from TRA/TPB.
Perceived BehavioralControlAdapted from TRA/TPB.
Perceived UsefulnessAdapted from TAM.

Table 1. Models and Theories of Individual Acceptance (Continued)Model of PC Utilization (MPCU) Core Constructs Definitions
Model of PC Utilization (MPCU)Core Constructs
Derived largely from Triandis' (1977) theory of humanbehavior, this model presents a competing perspectiveto that proposed by TRA and TPB. Thompson et al.(1991) adapted and refined Triandis' model for IS con-texts and used the model to predict PC utilization.However, the nature of the model makes it particularlysuited to predict individual acceptance and use of arange of information technologies. Thompson et al.(1991) sought to predict usage behavior rather thanintention; however, in keeping with the theory's roots,the current research will examine the effect of thesedeterminants on intention. Also, such an examination isimportant to ensure a fair comparison of the differentmodels.Job-fit"the extent to which an individual believes that using [atechnology] can enhance the performance of his or herjob" (Thompson et al. 1991, p. 129).
ComplexityBased on Rogers and Shoemaker (1971), "the degree towhich an innovation is perceived as relatively difficult tounderstand and use" (Thompson et al. 1991, p. 128).
Long-termConsequences"Outcomes that have a pay-off in the future" (Thompson etal. 1991, p. 129).
Affect Towards UseBased on Triandis, affect toward use is "feelings of joy,elation, or pleasure, or depression, disgust, displeasure,or hate associated by an individual with a particular act"(Thompson et al. 1991, p. 127).
Social FactorsDerived from Triandis, social factors are "the individual'sinternalization of the reference group's subjective culture,and specific interpersonal agreements that the individualhas made with others, in specific social situations"(Thompson et al. 1991, p. 126).
Facilitating ConditionsObjective factors in the environment that observers agreemake an act easy to accomplish. For example, returningitems purchased online is facilitated when no fee ischarged to return the item. In an IS context, "provision ofsupport for users of PCs may be one type of facilitatingcondition that can influence system utilization" (Thompsonet al. 1991, p. 129).

Table 1. Models and Theories of Individual Acceptance (Continued)Innovation Diffusion Theory (IDT) Core Constructs Definitions
Innovation Diffusion Theory (IDT)Core Constructs
Grounded in sociology, IDT (Rogers 1995) has beenused since the 1960s to study a variety of innovations,ranging from agricultural tools to organizationalinnovation (Tornatzky and Klein 1982). Withininformation systems, Moore and Benbasat (1991)adapted the characteristics of innovations presented inRogers and refined a set of constructs that could beused to study individual technology acceptance. Mooreand Benbasat (1996) found support for the predictivevalidity of these innovation characteristics (see alsoAgarwal and Prasad 1997, 1998; Karahanna et al. 1999;Plouffe et al. 2001).Relative Advantage"the degree to which an innovation is perceived as beingbetter than its precursor" (Moore and Benbasat 1991, p.195).
Ease of Use"the degree to which an innovation is perceived as beingdifficult to use" (Moore and Benbasat 1991, p. 195).
Image"The degree to which use of an innovation is perceived toenhance one's image or status in one's social system"(Moore and Benbasat 1991, p. 195).
VisibilityThe degree to which one can see others using the systemin the organization (adapted from Moore and Benbasat1991).
Compatibility"the degree to which an innovation is perceived as beingconsistent with the existing values, needs, and pastexperiences of potential adopters" (Moore and Benbasat1991, p. 195).
Results Demon-strability"the tangibility of the results of using the innovation,including their observability and communicability" (Mooreand Benbasat 1991, p. 203).
Voluntariness of Use"the degree to which use of the innovation is perceived asbeing voluntary, or of free will" (Moore and Benbasat1991, p. 195).

Table 1. Models and Theories of Individual Acceptance (Continued)Social Cognitive Theory (SCT) Core Constructs Definitions
Social Cognitive Theory (SCT)Core Constructs
One of the most powerful theories of human behavior issocial cognitive theory (see Bandura 1986). Compeauand Higgins (1995b) applied and extended SCT to thecontext of computer utilization (see also Compeau et al.1999); while Compeau and Higgins (1995a) also em-ployed SCT, it was to study performance and thus isoutside the goal of the current research. Compeau andHiggins' (1995b) model studied computer use but thenature of the model and the underlying theory allow it tobe extended to acceptance and use of informationtechnology in general. The original model of Compeauand Higgins (1995b) used usage as a dependentvariable but in keeping with the spirit of predictingindividual acceptance, we will examine the predictivevalidity of the model in the context of intention andusage to allow a fair comparison of the models.Outcome Expec-tations—PerformanceThe performance-related consequences of the behavior.Specifically, performance expectations deal with job-related outcomes (Compeau and Higgins 1995b).
OutcomeExpectations-PersonalThe personal consequences of the behavior. Specifically,personal expectations deal with the individual esteem andsense of accomplishment (Compeau and Higgins 1995b).
Self-efficacyJudgment of one's ability to use a technology (e.g.,computer) to accomplish a particular job or task.
AffectAn individual's liking for a particular behavior (..,computer use).
AnxietyEvoking anxious or emotional reactions when it comes toperforming a behavior (e.g., using a computer).

layout_dets.text: Settings and Participants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Measurement

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Longitudinal field studies were conducted at four

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: organizations among individuals being introduced

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to a new technology in the workplace. To help

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ensure our results would be robust across

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: contexts, we sampled for heterogeneity across

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: technologies, organizations, industries, business

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: functions, and nature of use (voluntary vs.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mandatory). In addition, we captured perceptions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: as the users' experience with the technology

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: increased. At each firm, we were able to time our

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: data collection in conjunction with a training

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: program associated with the new technology

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: introduction. This approach is consistent with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: prior training and individual acceptance research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: where individual reactions to a new technology

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were studied (e.g., Davis et al. 1989; Olfman and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Mandviwalla 1994; Venkatesh and Davis 2000).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A pretested questionnaire containing items mea-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: suring constructs from all eight models was

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: administered at three different points in time:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: post-training (T1), one month after implementation

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (T2), and three months after implementation (T3).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Actual usage behavior was measured over the six-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: month post-training period. Table 4 summarizes

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: key characteristics of the organizational settings.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Figure 2 presents the longitudinal data collection

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: schedule.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Eight Models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Voluntary vs. mandatory contexts: Most of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the model tests and all four model com-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: parisons were conducted in voluntary usage

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: contexts.³ Therefore, one must use caution

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: when generalizing those results to the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A questionnaire was created with items validated

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: in prior research adapted to the technologies and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: organizations studied. TRA scales were adapted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from Davis et al. (1989); TAM scales were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: adapted from Davis (1989), Davis et al. (1989),

layout_dets.text: 3Notable exceptions are TRA (Hartwick and Barki 1994)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: studies that have incorporated voluntariness as a direct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: effect (on intention) in order to account for perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nonvoluntary adoption (e.g., Agarwal and Prasad 1997;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Karahanna et al. 1999; Moore and Benbasat 1991).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Participants: While there have been some

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tests of each model in organizational settings,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the participants in three of the four model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: comparison studies have been students—

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: only Plouffe et al. (2001) conducted their

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research in a nonacademic setting. This

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research is conducted using data collected

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from employees in organizations.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly Vol. 27 No. 3/September 2003

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Timing of measurement: In general, most of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the tests of the eight models were conducted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: well after the participants' acceptance or

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: rejection decision rather than during the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: active adoption decision-making process.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Because behavior has become routinized,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: individual reactions reported in those studies

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: are retrospective (see Fiske and Taylor 1991;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Venkatesh et al. 2000). With the exception of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Davis et al. (1989), the model comparisons

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: examined technologies that were already

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: familiar to the individuals at the time of mea-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: surement. In this paper, we examine techno-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: logies from the time of their initial introduction

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to stages of greater experience.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Nature of measurement: Even studies that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: have examined experience have typically

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: employed cross-sectional and/or between-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: subjects comparisons (e.g., Davis et al. 1989;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Karahanna et al. 1999; Szajna 1996; Taylor

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Todd 1995a; Thompson et al. 1994).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: This limitation applies to model comparison

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: studies also. Our work tracks participants

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: through various stages of experience with a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: new technology and compares all models on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: all participants.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mandatory settings that are possibly of more

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: interest to practicing managers. This re-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: search examines both voluntary and man-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: datory implementation contexts.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Venkatesh and Davis (2000); MM scales were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: adapted from Davis et al. (1992); TPB/DTPB

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: scales were adapted from Taylor and Todd

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1995a, 1995b); MPCU scales were adapted from

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thompson et al. (1991); IDT scales were adapted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from Moore and Benbasat (1991); and SCT scales

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were adapted from Compeau and Higgins (1995a,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1995b) and Compeau et al. (1999). Behavioral

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: intention to use the system was measured using

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a three-item scale adapted from Davis et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1989) and extensively used in much of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: previous individual acceptance research. Seven-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: point scales were used for all of the aforemen-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tioned constructs' measurement, with 1 being the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: negative end of the scale and 7 being the positive

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: end of the scale. In addition to these measures,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: perceived voluntariness was measured as a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: manipulation check per the scale of Moore and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Benbasat (1991), where 1 was nonvoluntary and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 7 was completely voluntary. The tense of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: verbs in the various scales reflected the timing of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: measurement: future tense was employed at T1,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: present tense was employed at T2 and T3 (see

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Karahanna et al. 1999). The scales used to mea-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sure the key constructs are discussed in a later

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: section where we perform a detailed comparison

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Tables 9 through 13). A focus group of five

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: business professionals evaluated the question-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: naire, following which minor wording changes

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were made. Actual usage behavior was mea-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sured as duration of use via system logs. Due to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the sensitivity of usage measures to network

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Figure 2. Longitudinal Data Collection Schedule

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Results

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: availability, in all organizations studied, the system

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: automatically logged off inactive users after a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: period of 5 to 15 minutes, eliminating most idle

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: time from the usage logs.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The interested reader is referred to a more detailed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: exposition of bootstrapping and how it compares to other

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: techniques of resampling such as jackknifing (see Chin

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1998; Efron and Gong 1983).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Partial least squares (PLS Graph, Version

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 2.91.03.04) was used to examine the reliability

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and validity of the measures. Specifically, 48

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: separate validity tests (two studies, eight models,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: three time periods each) were run to examine

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: convergent and discriminant validity. In testing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the various models, only the direct effects on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: intention were modeled as the goal was to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: examine the prediction of intention rather than

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: interrelationships among determinants of inten-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion; further, the explained variance (R²) is not

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: affected by indirect paths. The loading pattern

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: was found to be acceptable with most loadings

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: being .70 or higher. All internal consistency

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reliabilities were greater than .70. The patterns of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: results found in the current work are highly con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sistent with the results of previous research.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The perceptions of voluntariness were very high in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: studies 1a and 1b (1a: M = 6.50, SD = 0.22; 1b:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: M = 6.51, SD = 0.20) and very low in studies 2a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: SD = 0.18). Given this bi-modal distribution in the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: data (voluntary vs. mandatory), we created two

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: data sets: (1) studies 1a and 1b, and (2) studies

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 2a and 2b. This is consistent with Venkatesh and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Davis (2000).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: PLS was used to test all eight models at the three

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: points of measurement in each of the two data

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: sets. In all cases, we employed a bootstrapping

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: method (500 times) that used randomly selected

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: subsamples to test the PLS model.4 Tables 5 and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 6 present the model validation results at each of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the points of measurement. The tables report the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: variance explained and the beta coefficients. Key

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Because pooling across time periods allows the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: explicit modeling of the moderating role of exper-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ience, there is an increase in the variance ex-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plained in the case of TAM2 (Table 7) compared

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to a main effects-only model reported earlier

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Tables 5 and 6). One of the limitations of pooling

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is that there are repeated measures from the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly Vol. 27 No. 3/September 2003

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Following the test of the baseline/original specifi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cations of the eight models (Tables 5 and 6), we

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: examined the moderating influences suggested

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (either explicitly or implicitly) in the literature—i.e.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experience, voluntariness, gender, and age

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Table 2). In order to test these moderating influ-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ences, stay true to the model extensions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Table 2), and conduct a complete test of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: existing models and their extensions, the data

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were pooled across studies and time periods.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Voluntariness was a dummy variable used to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: separate the situational contexts (study 1 vs.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: study 2); this approach is consistent with previous

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research (Venkatesh and Davis 2000). Gender

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: was coded as a 0/1 dummy variable consistent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with previous research (Venkatesh and Morris

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 2000) and age was coded as a continuous vari-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: able, consistent with prior research (Morris and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Venkatesh 2000). Experience was operationa-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: lized via a dummy variable that took ordinal values

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of 0, 1, or 2 to capture increasing levels of user

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experience with the system (T1, T2, and T3).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Using an ordinal dummy variable, rather than

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: categorical variables, is consistent with recent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research (e.g., Venkatesh and Davis 2000).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Pooling the data across the three points of mea-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: surement resulted in a sample of 645 (215 x 3).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The results of the pooled analysis are shown in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Table 7.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: findings emerged from these analyses. First, all

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: eight models explained individual acceptance,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with variance in intention explained ranging from

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 17 percent to 42 percent. Also, a key difference

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: across studies stemmed from the voluntary vs.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mandatory settings—in mandatory settings (study

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 2), constructs related to social influence were

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant whereas in the voluntary settings (study

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ), they were not significant. Finally, the deter-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: minants of intention varied over time, with some

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: determinants going from significant to nonsigni-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ficant with increasing experience.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: However, cross-sectional analysis using Chow's

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1960) test of beta differences (p < .05) from each

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: time period (not shown here) confirmed the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: pattern of results shown in Table 7. Those beta

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: differences with a significance of p < .05 or better

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (when using Chow's test) are discussed in the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: "Explanation" column in Table 7. The interaction

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: terms were modeled as suggested by Chin et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (1996) by creating interaction terms that were at

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the level of the indicators. For example, if latent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: variable A is measured by four indicators (A1, A2,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A3, and A4) and latent variable B is measured by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: three indicators (B1, B2, and B3), the interaction

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: term A × B is specified by 12 indicators, each one

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a product term—i.e., A1 × B1, A1 × B2, A1 × B3,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A2 × B1, etc.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: With the exception of MM and SCT, the predictive

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: validity of the models increased after including the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: moderating variables. For instance, the variance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: explained by TAM2 increased to 53 percent and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TAM including gender increased to 52 percent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: when compared to approximately 35 percent in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: In looking at technology use as the dependent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: variable, in addition to intention as a key predictor,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TPB and DTPB employ perceived behavioral

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: control as an additional predictor. MPCU employs

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: facilitating conditions, a construct similar to per-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ceived behavioral control, to predict behavior.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thus, intention and perceived behavioral control

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: were used to predict behavior in the subsequent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly Vol. 27 No. 3/September 2003

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cross-sectional tests of TAM (without moderators).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The explained variance of TRA, TPB/DTPB,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MPCU, and IDT also improved. For each model,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: we have only included moderators previously

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tested in the literature. For example, in the case

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of TAM and its variations, the extensive prior

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: empirical work has suggested a larger number of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: moderators when compared to moderators sug-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gested for other models. This in turn may have

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: unintentionally biased the results and contributed

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to the high variance explained in TAM-related

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: models when compared to the other models.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Regardless, it is clear that the extensions to the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: various models identified in previous research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mostly enhance the predictive validity of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: various models beyond the original specifications.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The significance of the interaction terms was also verified using Chow's test.

layout_dets.text: Seven constructs appeared to be significant direct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of the individual models (Tables 5 and 6). Of

layout_dets.text: so on (see Table 8). Since intention was used to

layout_dets.text: with increasing experience (at T3) indicating that

layout_dets.text: continued use could be directly hindered or

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: fostered by resources and opportunities. A nearly

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: identical pattern of results was found when the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (from MPCU) in place of perceived behavioral

layout_dets.text: control (the specific results are not shown here).

layout_dets.text: in MM, job-fit in MPCU, relative advantage in IDT,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.text: and C-TAM-TPB, perceived ease of use in TAM/

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TAM2, complexity in MPCU, ease of use in IDT,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and self-efficacy and anxiety in SCT. Finally, the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: C-TAM-TPB and TAM2), social factors (MPCU),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and image (IDT) were only significant in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Having reviewed and empirically compared the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: unified theory of acceptance and use of techno-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: logy (UTAUT). Toward this end, we examine com-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: monalities across models as a first step. Tables

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant in all time periods and that construct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Performance Expectancy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Performance expectancy is defined as the degree

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to which an individual believes that using the sys-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tem will help him or her to attain gains in job

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The performance expectancy construct within

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: each individual model (Table 9) is the strongest

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: predictor of intention and remains significant at all

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: points of measurement in both voluntary and man-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: datory settings (Tables 5, 6, and 7), consistent

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with previous model tests (Agarwal and Prasad

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: MIS Quarterly Vol. 27 No. 3/September 2003

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant role as direct determinants of user

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: acceptance and usage behavior: performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy, effort expectancy, social influence,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and facilitating conditions. As will be explained

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: below, attitude toward using technology, self-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: efficacy, and anxiety are theorized not to be direct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: determinants of intention. The labels used for the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: constructs describe the essence of the construct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and are meant to be independent of any particular

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: theoretical perspective. In the remainder of this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: section, we define each of the determinants,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: specify the role of key moderators (gender, age,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: voluntariness, and experience), and provide the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: theoretical justification for the hypotheses.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Figure 3 presents the research model.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: performance. The five constructs from the dif-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ferent models that pertain to performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy are perceived usefulness (TAM/TAM2

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and C-TAM-TPB), extrinsic motivation (MM), job-fit

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (MPCU), relative advantage (IDT), and outcome

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectations (SCT). Even as these constructs

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: evolved in the literature, some authors acknowl-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: edged their similarities: usefulness and extrinsic

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: motivation (Davis et al. 1989, 1992), usefulness

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and job-fit (Thompson et al. 1991), usefulness and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: relative advantage (Davis et al. 1989; Moore and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Benbasat 1991; Plouffe et al. 2001), usefulness

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and outcome expectations (Compeau and Higgins

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1995b; Davis et al. 1989), and job-fit and outcome

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectations (Compeau and Higgins 1995b).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The effort expectancy construct within each model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Table 10) is significant in both voluntary and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mandatory usage contexts; however, each one is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant only during the first time period (post-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: training, T1), becoming nonsignificant over

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: periods of extended and sustained usage (see

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Tables 5, 6, and 7), consistent with previous

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research (e.g., Agarwal and Prasad 1997, 1998;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Davis et al. 1989; Thompson et al. 1991, 1994).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Effort-oriented constructs are expected to be be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: more salient in the early stages of a new behavior,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: when process issues represent hurdles to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: overcome, and later become overshadowed by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: instrumentality concerns (Davis et al. 1989;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Szajna 1996; Venkatesh 1999).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Effort expectancy is defined as the degree of ease

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: associated with the use of the system. Three con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: structs from the existing models capture the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: concept of effort expectancy: perceived ease of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use (TAM/TAM2), complexity (MPCU), and ease

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of use (IDT). As can be seen in Table 10, there is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: substantial similarity among the construct defini-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tions and measurement scales. The similarities

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: among these constructs have been noted in prior

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research (Davis et al. 1989; Moore and Benbasat

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1991; Plouffe et al. 2001; Thompson et al. 1991).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Similar to gender, age is theorized to play a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: moderating role. Research on job-related atti-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tudes (e.g., Hall and Mansfield 1975; Porter 1963)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: suggests that younger workers may place more

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: importance on extrinsic rewards. Gender and age

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: differences have been shown to exist in techno-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: logy adoption contexts also (Morris and Venkatesh

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 2000; Venkatesh and Morris 2000). In looking at

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gender and age effects, it is interesting to note

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: that Levy (1988) suggests that studies of gender

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: differences can be misleading without reference to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: age. For example, given traditional societal gen-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: der roles, the importance of job-related factors

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: may change significantly (e.g., become sup-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: planted by family-oriented responsibilities) for

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: working women between the time that they enter

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the labor force and the time they reach child-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: rearing years (e.g., Barnett and Marshall 1991).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thus, we expect that the influence of performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy will be moderated by both gender and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: age.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Venkatesh and Morris (2000), drawing upon other

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: research (e.g., Bem and Allen 1974; Bozionelos

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1996), suggest that effort expectancy is more

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: salient for women than for men. As noted earlier,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the gender differences predicted here could be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: driven by cognitions related to gender roles (e.g.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Lynott and McCandless 2000; Motowidlo 1982;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Wong et al. 1985). Increased age has been

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: shown to be associated with difficulty in pro-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cessing complex stimuli and allocating attention to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: information on the job (Plude and Hoyer 1985),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: both of which may be necessary when using

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: software systems. Prior research supports the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: notion that constructs related to effort expectancy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: will be stronger determinants of individuals' inten-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tion for women (Venkatesh and Morris 2000;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Venkatesh et al. 2000) and for older workers

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Morris and Venkatesh 2000). Drawing from the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: arguments made in the context of performance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy, we expect gender, age, and exper-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ience to work in concert (see Levy 1988). Thus,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: we propose that effort expectancy will be most

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: salient for women, particularly those who are older

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and with relatively little experience with the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: system.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: H2: The influence of effort expectancy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: on behavioral intention will be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: moderated by gender, age, and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experience, such that the effect will

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be stronger for women, particularly

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: younger women, and particularly at

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: early stages of experience.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The role of social influence in technology

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: acceptance decisions is complex and subject to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a wide range of contingent influences. Social

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: influence has an impact on individual behavior

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: through three mechanisms: compliance, inter-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ture and/or causing an individual to respond to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: potential social status gains, the compliance

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mechanism causes an individual to simply alter

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: his or her intention in response to the social

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: pressure—i.e., the individual intends to comply

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with the social influence. Prior research sug-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: gests that individuals are more likely to comply

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behavior or punish nonbehavior (e.g., French

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Raven 1959; Warshaw 1980). This view of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: compliance is consistent with results in the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: technology acceptance literature indicating that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: reliance on others' opinions is significant only in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: mandatory settings (Hartwick and Barki 1994),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: particularly in the early stages of experience,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: when an individual's opinions are relatively ill-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: informed (Agarwal and Prasad 1997; Hartwick

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Barki 1994; Karahanna et al. 1999; Taylor

layout_dets.text: system.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Facilitating conditions are defined as the degree

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to which an individual believes that an organi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: zational and technical infrastructure exists to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: support use of the system. This definition cap-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tures concepts embodied by three different con-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: structs: perceived behavioral control (TPB/

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: DTPB, C-TAM-TPB), facilitating conditions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (MPCU), and compatibility (IDT). Each of these

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: constructs is operationalized to include aspects

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of the technological and/or organizational envi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ronment that are designed to remove barriers to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: use (see Table 12). Taylor and Todd (1995b)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: acknowledged the theoretical overlap by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: modeling facilitating conditions as a core com-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ponent of perceived behavioral control in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TPB/DTPB. The compatibility construct from

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: IDT incorporates items that tap the fit between

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: system in the organization.

layout_dets.text: Theory suggests that women tend to be more

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: an intention to use new technology (Miller 1976;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Venkatesh et al. 2000), with the effect declining

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with experience (Venkatesh and Morris 2000).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: As in the case of performance and effort expec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tancies, gender effects may be driven by psy-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: chological phenomena embodied within socially-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: constructed gender roles (e.g., Lubinski et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1983). Rhodes' (1983) meta-analytic review of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: age effects concluded that affiliation needs in-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: crease with age, suggesting that older workers

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: are more likely to place increased salience on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: social influences, with the effect declining with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: experience (Morris and Venkatesh 2000).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Therefore, we expect a complex interaction with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: these moderating variables simultaneously influ-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: encing the social influence-intention relation-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ship.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: H3: The influence of social influence

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: older women, particularly in man-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: datory settings in the early stages

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: of experience.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The empirical evidence presented in Tables 5,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 6, 7, and 8 suggests that the relationships

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: between each of the constructs (perceived

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behavioral control, facilitating conditions, and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: compatibility) and intention are similar. Specifi-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cally, one can see that perceived behavioral

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: control is significant in both voluntary and man-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: datory settings immediately following training

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (T1), but that the construct's influence on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: intention disappears by T2. It has been

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: demonstrated that issues related to the support

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: infrastructure—a core concept within the facili-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tating conditions construct—are largely captured

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: within the effort expectancy construct which taps

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the ease with which that tool can be applied

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (e.g., Venkatesh 2000). Venkatesh (2000)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: found support for full mediation of the effect of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: facilitating conditions on intention by effort

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy. Obviously, if effort expectancy is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: not present in the model (as is the case with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TPB/DTPB), then one would expect facilitating

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: conditions to become predictive of intention.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Our empirical results are consistent with these

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: arguments. For example, in TPB/DTPB, the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: construct is significant in predicting intention;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: however, in other cases (MPCU and IDT), it is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: nonsignificant in predicting intention. In short,

layout_dets.text: impediments to sustained usage (Bergeron et al.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1990). Organizational psychologists have noted

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: that older workers attach more importance to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: receiving help and assistance on the job (e.g., Hall

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and Mansfield 1975). This is further underscored

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: in the context of complex IT use given the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: increasing cognitive and physical limitations asso-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ciated with age. These arguments are in line with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: empirical evidence from Morris and Venkatesh

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (2000). Thus, when moderated by experience and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: age, facilitating conditions will have a significant

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: influence on usage behavior.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The empirical results also indicate that facilitating

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: conditions do have a direct influence on usage

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: beyond that explained by behavioral intentions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: alone (see Table 8). Consistent with TPB/DTPB,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: facilitating conditions are also modeled as a direct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: antecedent of usage (i.e., not fully mediated by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Empirically, the attitude constructs present an

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: interesting case (see Tables 5, 6, and 7). In some

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cases (e.g., TRA, TPB/DTPB, and MM), the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: attitude construct is significant across all three

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: time periods and is also the strongest predictor of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behavioral intention. However, in other cases (C-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TAM-TPB, MPCU, and SCT), the construct was

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: not significant. Upon closer examination, the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: attitudinal constructs are significant only when

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: specific cognitions—in this case, constructs

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: related to performance and effort expectancies—

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: are not included in the model. There is empirical

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: evidence to suggest that affective reactions (e.g.,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: intrinsic motivation) may operate through effort

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy (see Venkatesh 2000). Therefore, we

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: consider any observed relationship between

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: attitude and intention to be spurious and resulting

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from the omission of the other key predictors

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (specifically, performance and effort expec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tancies). This spurious relationship likely stems

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from the effect of performance and effort expec-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tancies on attitude (see Davis et al. 1989). The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: non-significance of attitude in the presence of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: such other constructs has been reported in pre-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: vious model tests (e.g., Taylor and Todd 1995a;

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thompson et al. 1991), despite the fact that this

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: finding is counter to what is theorized in TRA and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: TPB/DTPB. Given that we expect strong relation-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ships in UTAUT between performance expectancy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and intention, and between effort expectancy and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: intention, we believe that, consistent with the logic

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: developed here, attitude toward using technology

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: power analysis in the results section.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.text: Constructs Theorized Not to Be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Direct Determinants of Intention

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: motivation8 (MM), affect toward use (MPCU), and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: affect (SCT). Table 13 presents the definitions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and associated scale items for each construct.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Each construct has a component associated with

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: generalized feeling/affect associated with a given

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behavior (in this case, using technology). In

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: examining these four constructs, it is evident that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: they all tap into an individual's liking, enjoyment,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: joy, and pleasure associated with technology use.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Attitude toward using technology is defined as an

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: individual's overall affective reaction to using a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: system. Four constructs from the existing models

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: align closely with this definition: attitude toward

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behavior (TRA, TPB/DTPB, C-TAM-TPB), intrinsic

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 8Some perspectives differ on the role of intrinsic

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: motivation. For example, Venkatesh (2000) models it as

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a determinant of perceived ease of use (effort

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: expectancy). However, in the motivational model, it is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: shown as a direct effect on intention and is shown as

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: such here.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Computer self-efficacy will not

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: have a significant influence on

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: behavioral intention.6

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Compute anxiety will not have

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a significant influence on beha-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: vioral intention.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: rated by age and experience,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: such that the effect will be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: stronger for older workers, par-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ticularly with increasing exper-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ience.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Although self-efficacy and anxiety appeared to be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: significant direct determinants of intention in SCT

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (see Tables 5 and 6), UTAUT does not include

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: them as direct determinants. Previous research

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (Venkatesh 2000) has shown self-efficacy and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: anxiety to be conceptually and empirically distinct

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from effort expectancy (perceived ease of use).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Self-efficacy and anxiety have been modeled as

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: indirect determinants of intention fully mediated by

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: perceived ease of use (Venkatesh 2000). Consis-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: tent with this, we found that self-efficacy and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: anxiety appear to be significant determinants of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: intention in SCT—i.e., without controlling for the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: effect of effort expectancy. We therefore expect

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: self-efficacy and anxiety to behave similarly, that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is, to be distinct from effort expectancy and to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: have no direct effect on intention above and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: beyond effort expectancy.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.text: alternative procedure to estimate an adjusted R². PLS

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: estimates a measurement model that in turn is used to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the data using hierarchical regressions in SPSs. We

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
